Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

**Lemons** are a very common citrus fruit variety[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) from the tree *Citrus limon*.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Like other citrus fruits, lemons have a dimpled peel. They are a few inches/centimeters in diameter,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) and can range from ovular to fairly round.[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) The skin is characteristically bright yellow when ripe and highly aromatic,[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) with (+)-limonene and citral contributing a lemony aroma.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) The pulp is also yellow and very tart,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4) with the juice containing about 5% citric acid,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) as well as a relatively high proportion of vitamin C. The flavor is generally characterized as fresh and bright.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Lemons may or may not contain seeds, depending on the cultivar.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)

A few sub-varieties of lemon exist.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) The **Lisbon** and **Eureka lemons** are very prevalent,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) and they are generally thought of as "standard". The **Meyer lemon** is a lemon hybrid that is sweeter and more aromatic than most, with thin skin and few seeds.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Look for lemons that are firm, heavy, and bright yellow,[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9) with no soft or browned spots. Left on the tree, they will last for quite a while.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) Once harvested, they are best kept cool—they will last weeks in the fridge.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Lemons are not commonly eaten straight,[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4) but they are very widely used in cooking (both sweet and savory) for their flavor and acidity.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) As the central flavor, the zest and juice are both used to flavor cakes, cookies, soufflés, beverages, confections, sauces, marinades, and more.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:4-11) The zest is particularly helpful when lemon flavor is desired without liquid or acidity.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9) Lemon can also play a supporting role as a flavor enhancer,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9)[[11]](#cite_note-:4-11) especially in preparations of other fruits and several kinds of sauce. Both zest and thin slices of the whole fruit can act as a garnish, the peel can be candied, and the whole fruit can be preserved in salt.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:7-7) The acidity helps when whipping egg whites, cooking sugar, making pastry, preventing fruit discoloration, tenderizing meat, and cutting through heavy and fatty foods.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9)

## Techniques

[edit | edit source]

To get the most juice from a lemon, try soaking the whole fruit in hot water for an hour or rolling the whole fruit several times on a hard surface.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

In many cases, lemon juice and vinegar can be exchanged for each other,[[11]](#cite_note-:4-11) though the flavor will be considerably different.
